rulings.Case Title: SENIOR AND DISABILITY ACTION ET AL VS. ALEX PADILLA
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For A Peremptory Writ Of Mandate
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing the Law and Motion Calendar for Wednesday, February 20, 2019, line 2. PETITIONERS SENIOR AND DISABILITY ACTION, ALICE CHIU, AMERICAN CIVIL LIBERTIES UNION OF NORTHERN'S Motion For A Peremptory Writ Of Mandate. Judge Schulman discloses that he is a former member of the board of directors and former member of the Legal Committee of the American Civil Liberties Union of Northern California. This will not affect his ability to fairly and impartially decide this matter. Hearing required. The hearing is specially set for Friday, February 22 at 1:30 p.m. If that date and time is not convenient for counsel, the parties may request the clerk to set the matter on another date at 1:30 p.m. Counsel should be prepared at the hearing to address the following questions: 1. Is state mandamus relief available to compel Respondent to perform a ministerial duty arising under federal law? 2. In view of the express private right of action provision of the National Voter Registration Act (52 U.S.C. § 20510(b)(2)), does a state court have concurrent jurisdiction over an action for an alleged violation of the Act, or is Petitioners' exclusive remedy a civil action in federal court for declaratory or injunctive relief? 3. If a state court does have concurrent jurisdiction over an alleged violation of the NVRA, does the 90-day written notice of violation prerequisite to suit provision of the Act (§ 20510(b)) apply to such an action? 4. If the Court concludes that the 90-day provision applies, is dismissal the appropriate remedy, given that more than 90 days elapsed between Petitioners' June 20, 2018 prelitigation demand letter and the filing of Respondent Secretary of State's brief on the merits on January 14, 2019? 5. The Secretary has represented that he is "continuing his deliberate process of considering additional offices for designation as voter registration agencies, including the offices recommended by the ACLU and DRC," and has argued that for this reason, the action is not ripe for adjudication. What is the nature and substance of that process, and when does the Secretary anticipate completing it? =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CACH, LLC VS. WILLIAM T TAYLOR
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Compelling Responses To Plaintiff'S Request For Production Of Documents; Compelling Responses To Written Interrogatories; Establishing Admissions; And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing the Law and Motion Calendar for Wednesday, February 20, 2019, line 16. PLAINTIFF CACH, LLC'S Motion For Order Compelling Responses To Plaintiff's Request For Production Of Documents; Compelling Responses To Written Interrogatories; Establishing Admissions; And Request For Sanctions. Plaintiff CACH, LLC's motion for order compelling responses to Plaintiff's request for production of documents, compelling responses to written interrogatories, establishing admissions, and request for sanctions is granted in part. Defendant William Taylor's discovery responses were due by November 20, 2018. Mr. Taylor did not serve responses by that date, nor did he respond to the discovery requests or to Plaintiff's meet and confer letter before the February 7, 2019 hearing on Plaintiff's motion. For that reason, Judge Pro Tem Wohl properly concluded that Plaintiff's motion to compel should be granted. (Code Civ. Proc. §§ 2030.260, 2030.290, 2031.260, 2031.300, 2033.250, 2033.280.) On February 13, 2019, however, after the Judge Pro Tem issued his report and recommendation, Defendant provided partial written responses to certain of Plaintiff's form interrogatories, requests for admissions, and request for production of documents, and produced three pages of documents. Defendant did not serve responses to Plaintiff's special interrogatories. In view of Plaintiff's belated partial responses, not later than five days after notice of the Court's order, Defendant shall: (i) serve upon Plaintiff verified responses to Plaintiff's special interrogatories 1 through 11; (ii) serve upon Plaintiff verified responses to Plaintiff's form interrogatories Nos. 102.1 and 115.2; and (iii) produce any remaining documents in his possession, custody or control requested by the requests for production of documents. The Court finds that before the hearing on this motion, Defendant served a proposed response to the requests for admission that is in substantial compliance with Code of Civil Procedure section 2033.220. (Code Civ. Proc. § 2033.280(c); St. Mary v. Superior Court (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 762, 776, 778.) Accordingly, the matters sought to be admitted in the requests for admission are not deemed admitted. Sanctions are mandatory under the circumstances presented here, where Defendant failed to serve timely discovery requests, failed to respond to the motion to compel or to appear at the hearing on that motion, and failed to establish that he acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust. (Code Civ. Proc. §§ 2030.290(c), 2031.300(c), 2033.280(c).) The Court awards monetary sanctions in the amount of $385, which reflect the reasonable attorneys' fees and costs incurred by Plaintiff in connection with the preparation and filing of the motion. No fees are awarded for attendance at the February 7 hearing, as the Court understands that neither party appeared at that hearing. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JEFFREY KLEIN VS. NEWELL & COMPANY, INC, DBA
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Order Determining Good Faith Settlement
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing the Law and Motion Calendar for Wednesday, February 20, 2019, line 20. CROSS DEFENDANT DAVID RAMIREZ'S Motion For Order Determining Good Faith Settlement. Cross-Defendant David Ramirez dba David Ramirez Construction's motion for an order determining good faith settlement is granted. The Court has carefully considered the declarations and documents filed by all three cross-defendants seeking good faith determinations in this case, particularly Plaintiff's interrogatory responses regarding the amount of their claimed damages and the report of Plaintiffs' expert Richard Avelar regarding the causes of the claimed damages and the costs to repair the identified water intrusion and associated damages. The discovery responses, expert report, and other declarations provide sufficient information to support a rough approximation of the Plaintiffs' total recovery and of the moving party's proportionate liability. The Court finds that the amount of the settlement is within the reasonable range of the settling tortfeasor's proportional share of comparative liability for the plaintiff's injuries, and that the other pertinent factors weigh in favor of granting the motion. (See Tech-Bilt, Inc. v. Woodward-Clyde & Assocs. (1985) 38 Cal.3d 488, 499.) Based on information available at the time of the settlement, it is reasonable to conclude that in light of the moving party's limited role in the construction of Plaintiffs' house and the apparent lack of connection between its work and the causes of the water intrusion and associated damages, the moving party had little, if any, potential liability for Plaintiff's claimed damages. (See Cahill v. San Diego Gas & Electric Co. (2011) 194 Cal.App.4th 939, 962-966 [settlement amount of $25,000 was within ballpark of good faith settlements under Code of Civil Procedure section 877.6, despite plaintiff's multi-million dollar damages claim, when plaintiff had little if any chance of recovery at trial].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: THOMAS MARSONER ET AL VS. FCA US LLC A DELAWARE LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Thomas Marsoner, Annabelle Darauya'S Motion For Attorney'S Fees, Costs And Expenses
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing the Law and Motion Calendar for Wednesday, February 20, 2019, line 18. Plaintiffs Thomas Marsoner, Annabelle Darauya's Motion For Attorney's Fees, Costs And Expenses. Plaintiffs' motion for attorney's fees is granted in the amount of $16,963.12. In addition, Plaintiffs' unopposed motion for reimbursement of costs and expenses is granted in the amount of $6,067.23. The Court finds the amount of time expended on the case by plaintiffs' counsel and the monetary charge for that time to be excessive and unreasonable in light of the modest amount in controversy and the relative simplicity of the issues, particularly given counsel's expertise in the area and the number of attorneys (at least ten) who billed time on the case. Further, although defendants' accepted section 998 offer contemplated that the parties would attempt to stipulate to a reasonable award of fees and costs, plaintiffs made no effort before filing their motion to meet and confer. As a result, the time spent in connection with the motion (which could have been avoided altogether, had the parties stipulated to an award) was not reasonably incurred. For these reasons, the Court has subtracted that time (totaling $6,020) from the lodestar, and has reduced the remaining amount by 25%. (See Save Our Uniquely Rural Community Environment v. County of San Bernadino (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1179, 1188 [trial court did not abuse its discretion in reducing lodestar by 80 percent].) The Court declines to award a positive multiplier. (See Hall v. FCA US LLC (E.D. Cal. May 21, 2018) 2018 WL 2298431 at *8 [declining to award multiplier to same counsel in Song-Beverly Act case where, among other things, case did not present novel or difficult questions of law or fact, there was no evidence that the nature of the litigation precluded other employment by the attorneys, and the contingent nature of the fee award was outweighed by the other factors].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: GILL REPROGRAPHICS INC. VS. CLINT WALSTON ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion And Notice Of Motion To Stay Proceedings
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing the Law and Motion Calendar for Wednesday, February 20, 2019, line 13. Defendants and Cross-Complainants Clint Walton and Synergy Reprographics, LLC's motion to stay this action pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 410.30(a) in favor of the earlier-filed action pending in Texas state court is denied. A trial court considering a forum non conveniens issue engages in a two-step process. The first step is to determine whether a suitable alternative forum exists. (National Football League v. Fireman's Fund Ins. Co. (2013) 216 Cal.App.4th 902, 917.) Where (as here) there is a suitable alternative forum, the court proceeds to the next step, consideration of the private interests of the parties and the public interest in keeping the case in California. (Id.) The private interest factors are those that make trial and the enforceability of the ensuing judgment expeditious and relatively inexpensive, such as the ease of access to sources of proof, the cost of obtaining attendance of witnesses, and the availability of compulsory process for attendance of unwilling witnesses. (Id.) The public interest factors include avoidance of overburdening California courts, protecting potential jurors who should not be called on to decide cases in which the local community has little concern, and weighing the competing ties of California and the alternate jurisdiction to the litigation. (Id.; see also Leadford v. Leadford (1992) 6 Cal.App.4th 571, 575.) The defendant, as the moving party, bears the burden of proof on a motion based on forum non conveniens. (Id. at 918.) The trial court's weighing and balancing of private and public factors is reviewed pursuant to an abuse of discretion standard. (Id.) Here, Defendants have not met their burden to show that the pertinent factors weigh in favor of staying this action in favor of the Texas action. First, while Mr. Walston apparently is now a Texas resident, he alleges in the cross-complaint that he worked in San Francisco from 2006 to 2017 and that his damages were the result of Cross-Defendants' actions while he was located in San Francisco. Further, Synergy, which joined in the motion, apparently has its principal place of business in San Francisco and regularly conducts business in California. Moreover, the gravamen of the action relates to the termination of Mr. Walston's relationship with Plaintiff Gill Reprographics, Inc. in 2017. Many witnesses to the parties' cross-claims against each other, as well as documents and other evidence, therefore are likely to be located in California and subject to the process of this Court. Second, California has a substantial interest in enforcing its policies against misappropriation of trade secrets, unfair competition, intentional interference with contract, and tortious interference with prospective economic relations allegedly arising out of conduct occurring in California and affecting the operation of a California business and its employees. Third, the Texas action was filed only two months before this action, and Defendants did not seek a stay of this action until they filed the instant motion in January 2019, shortly before the approaching March 22, 2019 hearing on Plaintiffs' motion for summary judgment or summary adjudication and April 22, 2019 trial date. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: NATIONAL RESPONSE TEAM LLC VS. ANDREW LAM ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File First Amendment To Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing the Law and Motion Calendar for Wednesday, February 20, 2019, line 14. PLAINTIFF NATIONAL RESPONSE TEAM LLC'S Motion For Leave To File First Amendment To Complaint. Plaintiff National Response Team, LLC's motion for leave to file a first amendment to the complaint is granted. California's policy of great liberality in permitting amendments to the pleadings at any stage of the proceedings favors granting the motion, and Defendants do not claim that they will be prejudiced by allowing the amendment. Defendants have not shown that the proposed first amendment contradicts an admission in the original complaint or is a sham pleading. A complaint may plead inconsistent causes of action. (See Berman v. Bromberg (1997) 56 Cal.App.4th 936, 944-947.) rulings.Case Title: KATHERINE ABELL ET AL VS. DIGNITY HEALTH, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, Alternately, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing the Law and Motion Calendar for Wednesday, February 20, 2019, line 9. DEFENDANT DIGNITY HEALTH MEDICAL FOUNDATION MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, Alternately, Summary Adjudication. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 1 of 2 *** Defendant Dignity Health Medical Foundation's motion for summary judgment is denied, and its alternative motion for summary adjudication is denied as to the first, fourth and fifth causes of action for violation of the California Equal Pay Act, sex discrimination in violation of FEHA, and failure to prevent discrimination in violation of FEHA. Summary adjudication is granted as to the second and third causes of action, for retaliation in violation of Labor Code § 1102.5 and FEHA, and as to the claim for punitive damages. A triable issue of fact as to whether the proffered reason for the pay difference between plaintiffs' rates of compensation and those of male co-workers doing a substantially similar job precludes summary adjudication of the first and fourth causes of action. While defendant relies on differing preemployment and prelicensing work experience as a bona fide factor unrelated to sex to justify the pay difference, evidence that defendant maintained a grade system based on licensing status and that plaintiffs were still paid less when they were in the Behaviorial Health Clinician (BHC) III position than male co-workers in a prelicensing BHC II position may lead a reasonable juror to infer that such experience was a pretext and was not consistent with business necessity as required under Lab. Code, § 1197.5(a)(1)(D). Evidence that plaintiffs were paid less than than defendant's stated policy, which called for them upon receiving their license to be placed at the lower end of the wage range for the BHC III position, is relevant to pretext. (Haynes Depo: 62-64). Finally, although defendant did not need to have a written formula to factor in prelicensing experience, there is a triable issue of fact whether defendant created that formula after the plaintiffs' complaint, and a reasonable juror might infer that the formula was created to justify the pay discrepancy which was not originally based on a legitimate bona fide factor. (Plaintiffs' UMF 53-55.) The same evidence is relevant for purposes of showing pretext for plaintiffs' FEHA sex discrimination claim. In addition, inconsistencies in use of the formula for factoring in relevant experience as well as defendant's failure to follow its own procedures may provide evidence of pretext. (Figari Decl. Ex. A-16, p. 651; Haynes Depo: 62-64). (See Moore v. Regents of University of California (2016) 248 Cal.App.4th 216, 239 [a defendant's failure to follow its own policies or procedures may provide evidence of pretext].) Triable issues of fact as to the underlying claim for sex discrimination in violation of FEHA precludes summary adjudication of the derivative claim for failure to prevent discrimination. *** End of Part 1 - Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 2 of 2 ***

rulings.Case Title: KATHERINE ABELL ET AL VS. DIGNITY HEALTH, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Dignity Health Medical Foundation Motion For Summary Judgment Or, Alternately, Summary Adjudication (Added For Purposes Of Entering Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Defendant Dignity Health Medical Foundation Motion For Summary Judgment Or, Alternately, Summary Adjudication *** Tentative Ruling - Part 2 of 2 *** Summary adjudication of plaintiffs' claim for punitive damages is granted. Plaintiffs have not shown clear and convincing evidence that Defendants committed or ratified an act of oppression, fraud, or malice, or evidence from which such an inference reasonably could be drawn. (Civ. Code § 3294(b).) Summary adjudication of plaintiffs' retaliation claims is granted because plaintiffs have failed to make a prima facie showing of any causal link between their protected activity and any alleged adverse employment action. It is undisputed that plaintiffs' pay rate did not decrease after they complained, plaintiffs were not denied a pay raise, they were not demoted, disciplined, written up or subject to harassment. (Defendant's UMFs 5, 9, 13, 14, 16, 17). Nothing in their terms and conditions of employment materially changed. Plaintiffs fail to show how the difference in pay rate between them and the male co-workers is causally related to their protected activity when this difference existed before the protected activity. (See Morgan v. Regents of University of Cal. (2000) 88 Cal.App.4th 52, 69 ["The retaliatory motive is proved by showing that plaintiff engaged in protected activities, that his employer was aware of the protected activities, and that the adverse action followed within a relatively short time thereafter." (emphasis added)].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. *** This concludes the Tentative Ruling *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: FREDERIK MARTIN TOBER VS. GLOBAL COLLECT SERVICES USA INC, A DELAWARE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Global Collect Usa, Inc., Ingenico, Inc.'S Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint (Added For Purposes Of Entering Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Defendants Global Collect Usa, Inc., Ingenico, Inc.'s Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 2 of 2 *** Defendants are alleged to be subsidiaries or successors of Plaintiff's former employer, GCS Holding B.V., which initiated the Amsterdam litigation regarding Plaintiff's classification in leaving the company. However, the filing of that litigation, which related solely to the Stock Plan, did not "alert the defendant in the second claim of the need to begin investigating the facts which form the basis for the second claim." (Id.) While the two causes of action need not be absolutely identical, the facts of the two claims must be either "identical or at least so similar that the defendant's investigation of the first claim will put him in a position to fairly defend the second." (Id. at 925; see also id. ["So long as the two claims are based on essentially the same set of facts timely investigation of the first claim should put the defendant in position to appropriately defend the second."].) The Amsterdam litigation focused exclusively on whether Plaintiff was a "good leaver" and therefore entitled to receive stock options under the Stock Plan; it had nothing to do with whether Defendants had paid him the wages and bonuses he claims he was due under the Bonus Plan. Further, Plaintiff delayed filing the lawsuit until January 2018, over a year and a half after the Amsterdam proceedings had concluded. For these reasons, equitable tolling does not apply. The second and third causes of action for contractual and economic interference fail as a matter of law because such claims may be brought only against a third party who was not directly affiliated with the contract or economic relationship at issue. (Applied Equipment Corp. v. Litton Saudi Arabia Ltd. (1994) 7 Cal.4th 503, 513 ["The tort duty not to interfere with the contract falls only on strangers-interlopers who have no legitimate interest in the scope or course of the contract's performance."]; Reeves v. Hanlon (2004) 33 Cal.4th 1140, 1152 n.6 [elements for intentional interference with prospective economic advantage are similar to contractual interference, including a requirement that the claim be brought against a third party].) The first amended complaint alleges that Defendants were each other's agents, and that they are the subsidiary and successor-in-interest of Plaintiff's former employer. In any event, these two causes of action are independently barred by the two-year statute of limitations. (Code Civ. Proc. § 339(1).) Plaintiff has not shown a reasonable probability that the defects in the First Amended Complaint could be cured by amendment. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. *** End of Tentative Ruling *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: FREDERIK MARTIN TOBER VS. GLOBAL COLLECT SERVICES USA INC, A DELAWARE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing the Law and Motion Calendar for Wednesday, February 20, 2019, line 15. DEFENDANTS GLOBAL COLLECT USA, INC., INGENICO, INC.'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 1 of 2 *** Defendants Global Collect USA, Inc. and Ingenico Inc.'s demurrer to Plaintiff Frederik Martin Tober's first amended complaint is overruled as to the first and fifth causes of action for breach of contract and breach of Business & Professions Code section 17200, and sustained without leave to amend as to the second, third and fourth causes of action for interference with contractual relations, willful failure to pay wages in violation of the Labor Code, and intentional interference with prospective economic advantage. As an initial matter, Plaintiff's first amended complaint relates back to his initial complaint. While the general rule is that an amended complaint that adds a new defendant does not relate back to the filing of the original complaint, "where an amendment does not add a 'new' defendant, but simply corrects a misnomer by which an 'old' defendant was sued, case law recognizes an exception to the general rule of no relation back." (Hawkins v. Pacific Coast Bldg. Products, Inc. (2004) 124 Cal.App.4th 1497, 1503; see also Mayberry v. Coca Cola Bottling Co. (1966) 244 Cal.App.2d 350, 353-54 ["[A]n 'exception to the general rule' has been formulated, which permits correction where the plaintiff has committed an excusable mistake attributable to dual entities with strikingly similar business names or to the use of fictitious names."].) Plaintiff has shown that he sued similarly named, but incorrect defendants and that the only change in amending his complaint was changing the names of the Defendants to reflect the correct entities. Because the first amended complaint rests on the same facts, injuries, and legal claims as the initial complaint and adds no truly "new" defendants, the requirements for relation-back are satisfied and the correct date for measuring statutes of limitations is January 31, 2018, not August 1, 2018. (Norgart v. Upjohn Co. (1999) 21 Cal.4th 383, 408-09.) The court does not reach Defendants' evidentiary objections to paragraphs 2 and 4 of the Bercovitz Declaration as the court only relies upon paragraphs 5 and 6 of the Declaration, the initial complaint, and the first amended complaint. Defendants' objection to paragraph 6 of the Bercovitz Declaration is overruled as it is a declaration of fact about Plaintiff's reason for amending the complaint, not a legal conclusion. Accordingly, there is sufficient basis for the court to conclude that relation-back applies. The first and fifth causes of action for breach of contract and breach of Business & Professions Code section 17200 have limitations periods of four years and the date of the breach alleged by the first amended complaint is May 2, 2014, the date Plaintiff's employment ended. (Code Civ. Proc. §337; Bus. & Prof. Code §17208.) Because January 31, 2018 is less than four years after May 2, 2014, these claims are not barred by the statute of limitations and demurrers to these claims fail. The fourth cause of action for willful failure to pay all wages due at termination is barred by the three-year statute of limitations of Code of Civil Procedure section 338. The limitations period was not equitably tolled until the end of the proceedings of the Amsterdam District Court on May 2, 2016. The three factors to be considered in deciding whether equitable tolling should be applied are (1) timely notice to defendant in filing the first claim; (2) lack of prejudice to defendant in gathering evidence to defend against the second claim; and (3) good faith and reasonable conduct by the plaintiff. (Collier v. City of Pasadena (1983) 142 Cal.App.3d 917, 924.) *** End of Part 1 - Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 2 of 2 ***

rulings.Case Title: OKAN PEKGIL VS. BEVMO HOLDINGS LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Petition And Petition To Compel Arbitration And Motion To Dismiss Or Stay Proceedings; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing the Law and Motion Calendar for Wednesday, February 20, 2019, line 10. DEFENDANT BEVERAGES & MORE, INC'S Petition To Compel Arbitration And Motion To Dismiss Or Stay Proceedings. Defendant Beverages & More, Inc.'s petition to compel arbitration of the claims asserted in plaintiff Okan Pergil's first amended complaint is granted and the action is stayed pending conclusion of the arbitration proceedings. Beverages & More has met its burden to show by a preponderance of the evidence that Mr. Pekgil electronically signed the Arbitration Agreement by completing an onboarding process which plaintiff accessed through his personal email account shortly after an email with a link was sent to him. (See Espejo v. Southern California Permanente Medical Group (2016) 246 Cal.App.4th 1047, 1062 [declaration authenticating electronic signature established existence of agreement to arbitrate]; Cal. Civ. Code §1633.9(a); Chavis Decl. 4-9, 11, Exhs. A, C, D). Although a non-signatory, Beverages & More can compel arbitration under the Arbitration Agreement as a third-party beneficiary, because the intent of the parties was to regulate resolution of employment-related disputes and Beverages & More, a subsidiary of the signatory BevMo!, Inc., is Mr. Pekgil's employer. (See Garcia v. GMRI, Inc. (C.D. Cal. May 17, 2013) 2013 WL 10156088, at *6 [parent company of employer could enforce arbitration agreement as a third-party beneficiary].) Beverages & More need not be named in the contract to be a third-party beneficiary. (Macaulay v. Norlander (1992) 12 Cal.App.4th 1, 7.) In addition, Mr. Pekgil is estopped from denying Beverages & More's right to arbitrate because his action is "intimately founded in and intertwined" with his employment relationship with Beverages & More, which is governed by the arbitration agreement. The arbitration agreement expressly covers statutory claims. (Garcia v. Pexco, LLC (2017) 11 Cal.App.5th 782, 787.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JON B. EISENBERG ET AL VS. DAVE JONES ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant California Department Of Insurance Demurrer To 2nd Amended Complaint (Added For Purposes Of Entering Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: Defendant California Department Of Insurance Demurrer To 2nd Amended Complaint *** Tentative Ruling - Part 2 of 2 *** The demurrer is overruled as to causes of action 7-12. Under Insurance Code §§ 12921.3 and 12921.4, the Commissioner has discretion to pursue a particular remedy or any remedy at all against an insurer accused of misconduct. (See Schwartz v. Poizner (2010) 187 Cal.App.4th 592, 597.) The Commissioner, however, has a mandatory duty under those provisions to notify a complainant of receipt of the complaint, investigate the complaint, and notify the complainant of the final action taken on that complaint. Plaintiffs allege that the Commissioner has acted arbitrarily and capriciously by not taking final action on their complaints within a reasonable time. (SAC, 25, 32, 55-62, 86, 95.) Plaintiffs' allegations implicate sections 12921.3 and 12921.4 because they relate to "insurer" misconduct. (SAC, 25.) The Commissioner's contention that it has taken final action on the complaints raises disputed factual issues that cannot be resolved on demurrer. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. *** End of Tentative Ruling *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JON B. EISENBERG ET AL VS. DAVE JONES ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing the Law and Motion Calendar for Wednesday, February 20, 2019, line 11. DEFENDANT CALIFORNIA DEPARTMENT OF INSURANCE'S DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT *** Tentative Ruling - Part 1 of 2 *** Defendant Department of Insurance's demurrer to the Second Amended Complaint is overruled. The demurrer is overruled as to causes of action 1-6. Insurance Code § 790.05 provides that whenever the Commissioner shall have reason to believe that an unfair practice is occurring and it would be in the public interest to issue an order to show cause, then the Commissioner shall issue an order to show cause. Plaintiffs allege that persons are violating Insurance Code §§ 14022 and 14022.5, the Commissioner has been apprised of specific evidence regarding these violations, and that the Commissioner has failed to investigate such evidence. Plaintiffs allege sufficient ultimate facts showing that the Commissioner is acting arbitrarily and failing to perform his mandatory duty. (SAC, 34, 36, 47-54.) "Although traditional mandamus will not lie to control the discretion of a public official or agency, that is, to force the exercise of discretion in a particular manner, ' "... [it] will lie to correct abuses of discretion, and will lie to force a particular action by the ... officer, when the law clearly establishes the petitioner's right to such action" ' " (Schwartz v. Poizner (2010) 187 Cal.App.4th 592, 598.) Further, "[w]hile a party may not invoke mandamus to force a public entity to exercise discretionary powers in any particular manner, if the entity refuses to act, mandate is available to compel the exercise of those discretionary powers in some way." (Ellena v. Department of Ins. (2014) 230 Cal.App.4th 198, 205.) The Commissioner cites Women Organized for Employment v. Stein (1980) 114 Cal.App.3d 133, 139-140 and argues that there is no mandatory duty under Ins. Code § 790.05. There, various consumer, minority, and women's organizations sought to compel officials from the real estate, insurance, and business and transportation state agencies " 'to ... gather the minimum data necessary for proper future industry regulation.' " (Id. at p. 137; italics omitted .) They cited a wide assortment of statutes that broadly described the official duty to achieve the maximum protection for the public. (Id. at pp. 138-139.) Hence, the petition requested an extraordinarily broad and vague order to gather data based on an equally broad and vague smorgasbord of duties scattered throughout three different codes. The Court of Appeal found that the statutes did not define specific duties subject to a traditional writ of mandamus. The instant complaint is distinguishable as plaintiffs identify one administrative agency (Department of Insurance), one law (Insurance Code § 790.05), and one straightforward remedy (OSC). The Legislature explicitly specified the procedure to be employed in the circumstances that plaintiffs allege. (Id. at 139-140.) *** End of Part 1 - Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 2 of 2 ***
